title: The Spark — Psychology Certifications
description: During COVID lockdown I found myself with time and curiosity. Two courses — Introduction to Psychology (Yale via Coursera) and Social Psychology with Honors (Wesleyan via Coursera) — changed the way I read people. The concepts of social influence, cognitive biases, and behavioral conditioning explained everything around me.

title: Behavioral Theory — CBT and Freud
description: B.F. Skinner's Cognitive Behavioral Theory gave me a structure for understanding decision-making in everyday contexts — food, money, digital behavior. Freud's psychodynamic perspective added a different layer: the unconscious motivations behind consumer behavior, social media use, and herd mentality.

title: The Domino's Signal — 13 Crore Records Exposed
description: In May 2021, the Domino's India data breach leaked approximately 13 crore customer records — order histories, addresses, phone numbers, delivery patterns. College-age users had distinct, repeated consumption patterns nobody thought they were broadcasting. The breach made visible what was always happening silently. This was the catalyst.

attachment.label: Economic Times — Domino's Data Breach Report

title: Paper 1 — Designing the Cognitive Questionnaire
description: Inspired by the Domino's signal and Skinner's behavioral theory, I designed a three-section questionnaire to map how people make decisions. The survey ran for exactly 7 days — May 4 to May 10, 2021 — targeting youth (15–20), adults (20–24), and millennials (25+) across social media platforms.

figures.caption: Paper 1 — Questionnaire Taxonomy. Three sections: Introductory (demographic), Choice-Based (behavioral scenarios), and Decision Making (cognitive preference). Distributed across social media platforms over 7 days to capture cross-demographic responses.

dataTable.caption: Paper 1 — Questionnaire Taxonomy
rows: Name · Age group (Youth/Adult/Millennial) · Social media platforms used
Giver/Matcher/Taker self-ID · Criminal forgiveness scenario · Argument behavior
First priority (Family/Friends/World/Nation/Self) · Choose Love/Peace/Wealth/Money · Social validation importance

title: Paper 1 Published — Springer IACC
description: The cognitive decision-making paper was published in the International Advanced Computing Conference proceedings (Springer CCIS). ML algorithms classified respondents into 5 distinct behavioral clusters. The largest cluster (41%) showed the merciful-silent type: people who forgive, stay quiet, and feel special about their lives. COVID had shifted values — family rose above success in measurable ways.

figures.caption: Paper 1 — Cluster Insights. Visualization of the 5 behavioral clusters. Cluster 1 (Merciful-Silent, 41.04%) and Cluster 3 (Family-First, 31.21%) together represent nearly 75% of respondents — a strong COVID-era signal of values shifting toward forgiveness and family.

dataTable.caption: Table 5 — Meaningful Insights from Obtained Data
rows: Merciful mindset · Feels special about life · Stays silent in arguments
Chooses peace · Stays silent · Forgives others' mistakes
Prioritizes family above all · Stays silent in arguments
Chooses success over love/wealth/peace · Stays silent
Feels special in life · Taker rather than Giver

title: Paper 3 — Designing the Consumer Behavior Survey
description: Concurrently with Paper 1 publication, I began designing a 30-question survey to map how social media usage drives online purchasing decisions across India. The survey ran for 14 months. Three age cohorts: Teen (11–19), Adult (20–40), Mature (40+). Out of 1,500 collected, 1,438 were verified.

title: Paper 2 — Nutritional Psychology Survey
description: During COVID lockdown, I re-administered an existing dietary habit survey to the MIT-WPU engineering cohort I had been tracking. The pandemic had just reshaped eating behavior on campus and the data confirmed it. This second wave of responses, layered on top of the 2018–2019 baseline, created a before-and-after dataset that would later be the backbone of the nutritional psychology paper.

figures.caption: Paper 2 — Survey Taxonomy. The questionnaire captured dietary frequency, BMI self-reports, sickness history, food quality preferences, and monthly food spend. The same instrument was administered pre-COVID (2018–2019) and post-COVID (2020), enabling direct behavioral comparison.

title: Paper 2 Published — Nutritional Psychology, 98% Accuracy
description: Published in the Springer ICEI 2022 proceedings. The nutritional psychology paper analyzed dietary shifts in MIT-WPU engineering students across a 2018–2020 window — pre and post-COVID. Decision Tree on sickness frequency hit 98% accuracy. Random Forest on BMI hit 96.53%. Pre-COVID, 50% ate healthy. Post-COVID, that rose to 83%+.

title: Paper 2 — Model Accuracy Breakdown
description: Five prediction targets were tested using classification and regression models. The headline 98% accuracy (Decision Tree on frequency of falling sick) validated the hypothesis that behavioral signals from food patterns could predict health outcomes with a degree of precision traditional dietary surveys never achieved.

barChart.caption: ML Model Accuracy by Target Variable

title: Paper 3 Published — IoB Consumer Psychology
description: The paper that brought the three-year track together. The 1,438-person pan-India survey on social media and purchasing behavior confirmed most of what the earlier work had pointed toward. Adults (20–40) drove 72.36% of all orders. Instagram dominated at 75% average. Time on social media had a direct gradient effect — more time correlated with more purchases.

attachment.label: CRC Press — Internet of Behaviors Book Chapter

figures.alt: Bar chart: Social media platform usage by age group
figures.caption: Fig 1 — Platform Usage by Age Group. Instagram leads across all segments (Adult 76.45%, Mature 73.72%, Teen 73.84%). LinkedIn shows strong adult adoption (67.24%). Snapchat: Adult 66.92%, Mature 68.88%, Teen 68.02%. Facebook ~50% across all groups.

figures.alt: Bar chart: Time spent on social media vs. purchasing influence
figures.caption: Fig 6 — Time on Social Media vs. Purchase Influence. Direct gradient: <1 hour = 38.53% buy-influenced; 1–2 hours = 40.05%; >2 hours = 45.08%. The No share drops as time increases (40.60% to 34.20%), confirming prolonged exposure raises purchase intent.

figures.alt: Bar chart: Home appliance purchase influenced by gender, age group, and social media
figures.caption: Fig 11 — Home Appliance Purchases by Gender x Age x Social Media Influence. Adult females: 30.20% influenced. Adult males: 31.07% uninfluenced. Mature females: 9.26% influenced. Teen females: 5.64% — a strong generational and gender split in social media's sway over high-value product decisions.

title: One Narrative, Three Papers
description: The three-year arc from the Yale certification to the published IoB paper — cognitive decision-making (Springer IACC 2021), nutritional psychology (Springer ICEI 2022), and consumer behavior under the IoB framework (CRC Press 2023). Three separate surveys, three different scales of analysis, one consistent thread: behavioral data is measurable and interpretable with the right methodology.
